text: Robotic ventral hernia. One of the greatest advancements with the use of robotics in general surgery has been the development of ventral hernia repair and abdominal wall reconstruction. Using the robotic system, we now have the capability to perform extensive adhesiolysis using flexible scissors and dissection hooks in a way not previously possible. We can now close primary hernia defects with the wristed instruments to improve the functional architecture of the abdominal wall and also create a situation where we can suture the mesh to the abdominal fascia with a precision not previously available. The result is a hernia patient with a much more functional repair, a patient with lower rates of hernia recurrence, and significantly less wound complications. In addition, there's markedly less pain postoperatively.
This particular case involved a patient with two defects, with the larger one closer to the umbilicus. It's typical of an incisional hernia that has developed following a previous operation.

The surgeon clears the peritoneum and preperitoneal fat to expose the posterior rectus fascia, preparing a surface for mesh placement against the abdominal wall. Using a monopolar hook, the surgeon begins closing the hernia defect with a zero-sized V-lock barb suture, which retains tension with each pass. Relaxing incisions may be made laterally in the posterior rectus fascia to reduce closure tension, and these are covered with mesh to reinforce the repair.

The surgeon introduces the Ethicon PhysioMesh through an eight-millimeter port after closing the defect. A needle is attached to the center of the mesh, which is then passed through the fascial closure and out through the abdominal wall, securing the mesh in place for suturing.

The surgeon uses endo-wrist instrumentation to suture the mesh to the abdominal wall, achieving secure fascial purchase with each bite. The circumferential suture placement is visible, replacing the need for trans-fascial suturing and surgical tack placement typically required in laparoscopic repairs.

In our own practice series, Out of two hundred and seventy repairs, we have had three recurrences, which is a one point one recurrence rate over the past three years, and a three point three rate of seroma formation, none of which have been clinically significant. In addition, there have been no serious wound infections.

Thus far in our experience, a very effective and safe approach to hernia repair. In addition, Since it is almost identical to suturing during an open procedure, the learning curve for new robotic surgeons is virtually non-existent. Operative times have been comparable with laparoscopic operative times as well as open procedures. One of the myths perpetuated about robotic surgery is that longer operating times and longer setup times are a necessary evil.

The video shows the completion of the hernia repair with a robust primary closure of the defect. The mesh is placed symmetrically and tension-free, without any overlapping, and a circumferential suture line is secured to the fascia.
